title: IV Fluid Therapy
summary: العلاج بالسوائل الوريدية يهدف إلى تعويض السوائل والشوارد المفقودة وتوفير السعرات الحرارية. تنقسم السوائل إلى كريستالويدات (متساوية التوتر، منخفضة التوتر، عالية التوتر) وكولويدات (محلول الألبومين 5%). اختيار السائل المناسب يعتمد على حالة المريض: محلول ملحي طبيعي للحجم، محلول ناقص التوتر لجفاف الخلايا، محلول فرط التوتر لنقص صوديوم الدم الشديد، والألبومين لتوسيع الحجم داخل الأوعية في الصدمة.

key_drugs.name: Normal Saline 0.9% NaCl
key_drugs.note: المحلول الملحي الطبيعي - يبقى في الفضاء خارج الخلوي

key_drugs.name: Ringer's Lactate (RL)
key_drugs.note: الأقرب لتركيب بلازما الدم - الخيار الأول للحروق والعمليات

key_drugs.name: Dextrose 5% (D5W)
key_drugs.note: يتحول لمحلول ناقص التوتر بعد استقلاب الجلوكوز

key_drugs.name: 0.45% NaCl (Half Normal Saline)
key_drugs.note: لعلاج الجفاف داخل الخلوي وفرط صوديوم الدم

key_drugs.name: 3% NaCl (Hypertonic Saline)
key_drugs.note: لعلاج نقص صوديوم الدم الشديد المصحوب بأعراض عصبية

key_drugs.name: 5% Albumin
key_drugs.note: يرفع الضغط الغروي ويوسع الحجم داخل الأوعية - للصدمة وضعف الألبومين

mnemonics.title: 3 أنواع الكريستالويدات - ISO HYPO HYPER
mnemonics.body:
ISO (متساوي التوتر) = Normal Saline 0.9% + Ringer's Lactate + D5W
- يبقى في الفضاء خارج الخلوي
HYPO (ناقص التوتر) = 0.45% NaCl, 0.33% NaCl
- يدخل إلى داخل الخلايا
HYPER (فرط التوتر) = 3% NaCl, 5% NaCl
- يسحب الماء من الخلايا إلى خارجها

mnemonics.title: قصة D5W - المخادع المتوتر
mnemonics.body: D5W يبدو متساوي التوتر (isotonic) كالجار الهادئ. لكن بمجرد دخوله الجسم، يُستقلب الجلوكوز بسرعة فيتحول المحلول إلى ماء نقي (ناقص التوتر) يدخل الخلايا! لذلك: لا يُستخدم للإنعاش لأنه لن يبقى في الأوعية، ولا يُعطى مع الدم (يُسبب تحلل كريات الدم)، وخطر على ارتفاع الضغط داخل القحف.

mnemonics.title: تصور حركة الماء بين المحاليل والخلايا
mnemonics.body:
تخيّل خلية في وسط ثلاثة أحواض:
- حوض متساوي التوتر (Normal Saline): الخلية هادئة لا تتغير
- حوض ناقص التوتر (0.45% NaCl): الماء يُسرع نحو الخلية ويُنفخها
- حوض فرط التوتر (3% NaCl): الماء يهرب من الخلية فتتقلص
هذا يُفسر لماذا نستخدم فرط التوتر لتقليص خلايا المخ في الوذمة الدماغية.

mnemonics.title: الألبومين - حارس الضغط الغروي
mnemonics.body: تخيّل جزيئات الألبومين كـ'مغناطيسات كبيرة' داخل الأوعية تسحب الماء من النسيج الخلالي إلى الدم. عندما تنخفض البروتينات (كما في الصدمة وضعف التغذية) يهرب الماء من الأوعية إلى الأنسجة فيحدث الوذمة. إعطاء 5% Albumin يُعيد هذه 'المغناطيسات' ويُوسِّع الحجم داخل الأوعية.

mnemonics.title: BIDS - مضاعفات ومحاذير D5W
mnemonics.body:
B = Blood - لا تخلطه مع الدم (تحلل كريات الدم)
I = ICP - لا تُعطِه لمرضى ارتفاع الضغط داخل القحف
D = Dialysis patients / cardiac - لا للفشل الكلوي والقلبي
S = Shock/Resuscitation - لا يُستخدم للإنعاش

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a crystalloid and a colloid?
flashcards.back: Crystalloid: clear solutions of water and electrolytes that cross cell membranes. Colloid: contains large molecules (proteins) that remain within the vasculature longer and raise oncotic pressure.

flashcards.front: What is the composition of Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl)?
flashcards.back: Contains only water, sodium (154 mEq/L), and chloride (154 mEq/L). Its concentration approximates that of these electrolytes in blood plasma.

flashcards.front: What is the complication of administering large volumes of Normal Saline?
flashcards.back: Hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis due to the high Cl- content, in addition to the risk of fluid overload.

flashcards.front: What is the advantage of Ringer's Lactate over Normal Saline?
flashcards.back: Ringer's Lactate more closely resembles normal plasma composition (contains Na, Cl, K, Ca, Lactate) and carries a lower risk of hyperchloraemic acidosis.

flashcards.front: Why does D5W become a hypotonic solution after administration?
flashcards.back: Because glucose is rapidly metabolised, leaving only water — an effectively hypotonic solution that enters cells.

flashcards.front: Why is D5W not given to infants?
flashcards.back: Because infants are more susceptible to dilutional hyponatraemia due to their limited renal capacity to excrete free water.

flashcards.front: What are the indications for hypotonic solutions?
flashcards.back: Hypernatraemia, diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS).

flashcards.front: What are the warnings for hypotonic solutions?
flashcards.back: Do not give to patients with raised intracranial pressure (risk of cerebral oedema), or to patients with liver disease, burns, or shock (risk of vascular volume depletion).

flashcards.front: What are the indications for hypertonic saline (3% NaCl)?
flashcards.back: Treatment of severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (coma, seizures) and cerebral oedema.

flashcards.front: What is the danger of rapidly correcting hyponatraemia?
flashcards.back: Over-rapid correction may cause cerebral oedema, seizures, and death. Therefore hypertonic saline must be given slowly via a central venous line.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism by which 5% Albumin expands intravascular volume?
flashcards.back: Albumin raises the oncotic pressure within the vasculature, drawing water from the interstitial space into the vessels.

flashcards.front: What are the indications for 5% Albumin?
flashcards.back: Intravascular volume expansion, moderate protein replacement, and haemodynamic stabilisation in shock states.

flashcards.front: What are the precautions for administering colloids?
flashcards.back: Use an 18-gauge needle or larger; monitor for signs of hypervolaemia (hypertension, dyspnoea, oedema); watch for rare anaphylactoid reactions.

flashcards.front: What are the indications for Normal Saline?
flashcards.back: Treatment of extracellular volume depletion (haemorrhage, vomiting, severe diarrhoea); drug dilution; dilution of packed red blood cells before transfusion.

flashcards.front: What are the fluid requirements in surgery?
flashcards.back: Includes: daily maintenance + deficit (hours fasted x maintenance rate) + third-space losses + blood losses.

flashcards.front: What is the osmolality of a hypotonic solution?
flashcards.back: Less than 250 mOsm/L. Normal plasma osmolality is 280-295 mOsm/L.

flashcards.front: What is the key message in IV fluid therapy?
flashcards.back: Treat intravenous fluids like a prescription drug — the wrong dose or wrong type can cause serious complications.

flashcards.front: What are the indications for IV fluid therapy?
flashcards.back: Replacement of fluids and electrolytes, provision of glucose for energy, drug and vitamin delivery, when oral intake is not possible.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Crystalloids and Colloids?
flashcards.back: Crystalloids: water and electrolytes that cross membranes (NaCl, Ringer's). Colloids: large molecules that remain within vessels longer (Albumin, Dextran).

flashcards.front: What is an isotonic solution?
flashcards.back: A solution with a particle concentration equal to that of plasma; it does not enter cells and remains extracellular, increasing intravascular volume. Example: NaCl 0.9%.

flashcards.front: What is the composition of Normal Saline 0.9% NaCl?
flashcards.back: Water + sodium 154 mEq/L + chloride 154 mEq/L (equivalent to plasma concentration).

flashcards.front: When is hypertonic solution used?
flashcards.back: Severe hyponatraemia; draws water from cells into the vasculature. Example: NaCl 3%.

flashcards.front: When is hypotonic solution used?
flashcards.back: Intracellular dehydration; water enters cells. Example: NaCl 0.45%.

flashcards.front: What is Ringer's Lactate solution?
flashcards.back: An isotonic crystalloid containing sodium, potassium, calcium, chloride, and lactate — used for volume replacement.

flashcards.front: What are the complications of excessive IV fluid administration?
flashcards.back: Oedema (fluid overload), cardiac failure, electrolyte disturbances.

mcqs.q: A patient with severe burns requires fluid resuscitation. Which solution is most appropriate?
mcqs.explanation: Ringer's Lactate is the optimal choice for burns as it most closely resembles plasma composition and contains K+, which is important in this setting.

mcqs.q: A patient with chronic heart failure develops dehydration from gastroenteritis. Which fluid must be used cautiously to avoid fluid overload?
mcqs.explanation: Patients with heart failure are at risk of fluid overload. Hypotonic solutions (0.45% NaCl) are less stable in the vascular space because the fluid enters cells.

mcqs.q: A patient has severe hyponatraemia with a seizure. What is the most appropriate fluid?
mcqs.explanation: Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures, coma) requires hypertonic 3% NaCl to rapidly raise serum sodium under close monitoring.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is correct regarding D5W?
options: It is used for resuscitation in haemorrhagic shock
It can be mixed with packed red blood cells
It becomes a hypotonic solution after glucose is metabolised
It can be safely given to patients with raised intracranial pressure
mcqs.explanation: After glucose is metabolised, only water remains; D5W becomes a hypotonic solution that enters cells. Therefore it cannot be used for resuscitation, mixed with blood, or given with raised ICP.

mcqs.q: What is the most appropriate fluid for a patient with intracellular dehydration due to diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)?
mcqs.explanation: DKA causes intracellular dehydration due to hyperosmolality. Hypotonic solution (0.45% NaCl) enters cells and rehydrates them.

mcqs.q: What pressure makes 5% Albumin solution effective in expanding intravascular volume?
mcqs.explanation: Albumin raises the oncotic pressure produced by large molecules (proteins) within the vasculature, drawing fluid from the interstitial tissue.

mcqs.q: A patient is given large volumes of Normal Saline for dehydration. What complication is expected?
mcqs.explanation: Normal Saline contains 154 mEq/L of Cl- (higher than plasma). Large volumes cause Cl- accumulation and hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a warning when administering hypertonic saline?
options: Administer rapidly via a small peripheral vein
Risk of hypervolaemia and pulmonary oedema; administer slowly via a central line
mcqs.explanation: Hypertonic saline draws fluid into the vasculature, potentially causing hypervolaemia and pulmonary oedema. It must be given slowly via a central venous line with close monitoring of serum sodium.

mcqs.q: A female patient is receiving 5% Albumin in the ICU for shock. Which sign requires immediate discontinuation?
mcqs.explanation: Dyspnoea, crackles, and oedema indicate hypervolaemia — a serious complication of colloids requiring immediate cessation of the infusion.

mcqs.q: What is the indication for Dextrose 5% (D5W)?
options: Patients unable to eat (NPO) to provide caloric intake
mcqs.explanation: D5W is used to provide calories for patients who cannot take oral nutrition (NPO patients) — it delivers 170 kcal/L. However it does not replace electrolytes and is not used for resuscitation.

mcqs.q: 100% bioavailability and a composition similar to plasma describes:
mcqs.explanation: An isotonic solution remains extracellular and increases intravascular volume.

mcqs.q: To correct severe hyponatraemia we use:
mcqs.explanation: Hypertonic saline raises sodium and draws water into the vasculature.
Albumin is a colloid that remains within the vasculature.
